posts.title: Installing Ubuntu onto my ThinkPad
posts.content: Recently, I purchased a used Lenovo ThinkPad X1 Carbon 9th Gen. with 16GM RAM and a 512GB SSD to begin learning Linux. However, since the ThinkPad came with Windows OS, the first task was learning how to install Linux as the operating system. I decided to go with the Ubuntu distro due to its reputation as being easy for beginners.While the official Ubuntu documentation for creating a bootable Ubuntu USB with a Mac provided a great starting point, there were many things that I had to research as well, which inspired me to write this blog to consolidate all of the information.Please note, these instructions assume you are completing the steps on a Macbook unless otherwise noted.To begin, I downloaded an Ubuntu ISO file from their official site and wiped an 8GB USB stick.To prepare the USB, I first removed any files that I wished to preserve. Next, I launched Disk Utility and clicked on the USB from the list of devices. From there, I set the format to MS-DOS (FAT) and clicked Erase to fully wiped the drive.Before clicking Erase, ensure that you have selected the right device to avoid accidental data loss.Next, I installed Etcher, an open-source program designed to safely and easily write OS files onto USB drives. Once Etcher was installed, I selected the Ubuntu ISO file I previously downloaded and the USB drive I wiped then hit Flash! to start the writing process. This took about 15–20 minutes to complete.After the writing process finishes, there may be a popup warning that Mac cannot read the USB drive. If this happens, do NOT click Initialize. Click Eject and remove the USB drive.Once the writing process is complete, I ejected the USB drive.Now that the bootable Ubuntu USB has been created, the next step is to install Ubuntu onto the ThinkPad.Before installing Ubuntu, ensure that any files or information that you wish to save from the laptop have been removed, as this process will completely overwrite everything on the computer.First, plug the USB into the ThinkPad and hold down on the power button until the screen goes completely black. When the Lenovo logo flashes onto the screen, hit the F12 key to open up the boot menu.Once the boot menu is opened, ensure the USB is selected and hit Enter. From there the Ubuntu OS will begin to install. I went through all of the setup windows and finished configuring my new Ubuntu laptop!To remove the Ubuntu OS from the USB and turn it back into a traditional USB drive, I plugged the bootable USB back into my Mac. Next, I opened up Terminal and entered the following command:diskutil eraseDisk MS-DOS UNTITLED MBRFormat /dev/disk4This command uses diskutil to erase and reformat the disk. After reformatting, it will use the MS-DOS filesystem, be called UNTITLED, and use the MBRFormat (Master Boot Record) disk format. /dev/disk4 was the device identifier for my bootable USB, which I found by opening Disk Utility, selecting the USB, and looking at the Device.Now that my ThinkPad is running Ubuntu and my USB drive is back to normal, my goal is to start working on projects that benefit from a Linux operating system.

posts.title: WhiteRabbitNeo Takes on RSAC 2025
posts.subtitle: WhiteRabbitNeo is going to RSAC! Come meet our team at one of the many events we are hosting both inside and outside the Moscone Center
posts.description: WhiteRabbitNeo is going to RSAC! Come meet our team at one of the many events we are hosting both inside and outside the Moscone Center
posts.content: WhiteRabbitNeo is going to RSAC! Come meet our team at one of the many events we are hosting both inside and outside the Moscone Centerby Bailey WilliamsWe’ve hacked Oasis Grill at 4th and Howard!Half a block from the doors of the Moscone Center, Oasis Grill (located at 200 4th St) is a standard at RSAC! Join us April 28 to 30, for free breakfast, lunch, and a quiet place to catch up from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. You can even test out our brand-new WhiteRabbitNeo model, released just in time for RSAC 2025!Join us for events at the Adversary Village Sandbox!With your Expo Plus Pass, you can join us for hands-on activities in the Adversary Village Sandbox at the Yerba Buena Center for the Arts (YBCA — located at 701 Mission St, just a 3-minute walk from the Moscone Center).On April 28th and 30th at 1 p.m. and 2 p.m., Andy Manoske (VP of Product at Kindo) will lead a hands-on workshop entitled AI Adversary Ops: Collecting threat intel and crafting attack emulation plans with open-source, uncensored AI in the Adversary Village Sandbox.In this hands-on workshop, participants will leverage the power of the WhiteRabbitNeo open-source generative AI model to power their cyber threat intelligence collection and attack emulation planning. Learn how to gather and analyze real-world threat actors, adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), then transform them into realistic, actionable attack scenarios. Through practical exercises, participants will see how uncensored AI can accelerate adversary emulation, enhance operations, and push the boundaries of offensive cybersecurity.On April 29th and 30th from 1 p.m. to 3 p.m., Bailey Williams (WhiteRabbitNeo) and Ken Kato (CSO at Kindo) will lead a workshop entitled AI-Assisted Cyber Incident Response Playbook Generation, which will run every 30 minutes in the Adversary Village Sandbox.In this hands-on workshop, participants will use WhiteRabbitNeo, an open-source generative AI model, to build a dynamic, effective incident response playbook to deal with real-world cyber threats. Using WhiteRabbitNeo’s speed and adaptability, participants will collect and analyze threat intelligence, map potential attack scenarios, and outline step-by-step response actions for each phase from detection and containment to eradication and recovery. Through interactive prompts and guided AI model collaboration, participants will craft a flexible, well-structured IR playbook designed to counter evolving adversary tactics and minimize organizational impact during an incident.On April 30th, from 9:40 a.m. to 10:50 a.m., on the Main Sandbox Stage, Ken Kato (CSO at Kindo) will be on the Building Cyber Defenses to Withstand Sophisticated Cyber Adversaries panel alongside Abhijith ‘Abx’ B R (Founder and Head of Security Consulting, Adversary Village | BreachSimRange), Jon Baker (Director, Center for Threat-Informed Defense, MITRE), and Vivek Ramachandran (Founder & CEO, SquareX).This panel will discuss the idea that cyberattacks ranging from sophisticated ransomware to state-sponsored breaches have targeted critical infrastructure and major organizations, driving escalation in security efforts. Despite advanced tools, no organization is immune to adversaries. This panel will review the key breaches of 2024, discuss ongoing issues, and strategies for improving incident response, containment, and defense.From Amazon, AI-Driven Transformation of the SOC and SecOps (2nd Edition) by Adeel Shaikh MuhammadLooking for exclusive, rabbit-themed swag? At Oasis Grill and the hands-on events within the Adversary Village sandbox, we will be giving out hoodies featuring rabbit ears, WhiteRabbitNeo white rabbit pins, and copies of AI-Driven Transformation of the SOC and SecOps (2nd Edition) by Adeel Shaikh Muhammad, which features WhiteRabbitNeo use cases!Want to talk to us outside of these events? Keep an eye out for our staff wearing black hoodies featuring white rabbit ears.

posts.title: Unveiling the Future of AI: A Masterclass on Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
posts.subtitle: Enhance your AI workflows with real-time information
posts.description: Enhance your AI workflows with real-time information
posts.content: Enhance your AI workflows with real-time informationBy Bailey WilliamsHave you ever noticed that AI responses are inaccurate when asked about current events or bleeding-edge developments like emerging technologies? This is because LLMs are limited to the knowledge they were given in their initial training set. The inability to update or version LLMs as we would most software is especially limiting in the fast-paced world of cybersecurity. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) fixes this by bridging the gap between the initial training data and the present.RAG addresses this limitation by dynamically updating the information the LLM references, including information on newly discovered vulnerabilities, patches, software updates, and evolving attack vectors. Real-time threat intelligence allows LLMS to respond to security incidents with significantly enhanced accuracy, mitigating the potential damage of cyberattacks.Let’s explore RAG in more depth by looking at different RAG methods and demonstrating how they revolutionize AI responses, improving accuracy and providing dynamic, real-time context.Understanding the Essence of RAGRAG enables LLMs to retrieve current information from various sources to respond more accurately to user queries. These sources include web search results, real-time information, and your own proprietary knowledge store. This dynamic retrieval process also helps to “ground” the LLM’s responses, reducing the risk of hallucinations by providing more relevant context for the LLM to pull from. The AI can also be configured to add links to the sources used in its response, which allows you to verify that the information came from sources you trust.You can think of the RAG process as similar to the process of writing a research paper, where first you create a research objective (the user query), then conduct a literature review by finding any sources relevant to your research objective and reviewing them (the RAG approach), and then you write your research paper using those sources to provide context and validity to your arguments (synthesis).RAG Approaches: Vector Embeddings and Graph DatabasesRAG can be implemented using three different approaches: Vector Embeddings, Graph Databases, and Tool Use applications. Below, we’ll dive into a high-level overview of these approaches and explain how all the information comes together to form the final response.Vector Embeddings: This technique transforms the way AI interacts with unstructured data. Unlike traditional search mechanisms that rely on exact matches, vector embeddings utilize semantic understanding to locate information.Each document is broken into smaller chunks, then embedded within a vector space using transformer architecture and plotted on vectors within the space. This spatial plotting allows semantically similar chunks to be grouped in the vector space. Using cosine similarity or similar vector search algorithms, user queries are embedded and plotted to find semantically similar document chunks. The LLM then pulls these chunks and ranks them to find the most relevant information for the user query.The LLM then uses the most relevant context to answer the prompt. Moreover, it automatically integrates document metadata, ensuring contextual coherence by retaining knowledge of the surrounding text. This enriches the AI’s understanding and response accuracy. Metadata can also be used for traditional filtering to further guarantee the relevancy of the data.This image visualizes how related entities are grouped to allow the LLM to find semantically similar chunks of data.Graph Databases: Similar to vector embeddings, this approach adopts a relational perspective, mapping entities and their interconnections within a graph structure. By delineating relationships based on the proximity between various data points, graph databases facilitate a nuanced search that identifies the most relevant entities in response to user queries. This relational mapping enhances the AI’s ability to comprehend complex interdependencies, providing a holistic and contextually rich response.This graph shows how relationships between the user query and embedded entities are mapped based on proximity.Tool Use: A final approach to RAG is interfacing it with external tools, expanding the LLM’s contextual framework beyond its training data. This application of RAG is different from vector embeddings and graph databases because it allows the LLM to take action in response to user queries. By understanding the functionalities of different tools, RAG creates API queries to extract information from these tools, using the tool’s response as context for the LLM’s response to the user query. For example, a tool-use program may query CVE records to explain a specific CVE, as shown in the image below. This allows the LLM to retrieve and synthesize external data, enhancing its responses.WhiteRabbitNeo (used within the Kindo chat) responds using URL search.In the above image, I’ve asked the WhiteRabbitNeo 2.5 32B (Beta) model within the Kindo chat interface (with URL search enabled) to explain “what is CVE–2024–1985?” To answer, WhiteRabbitNeo sent out API queries to gain context, which are then given as hyperlinks at the top of the response.Synthesis: The Final StepThe final step of the RAG process is the synthesis stage, which was alluded to in the overview of each RAG approach. Synthesis is when the LLM consolidates all the information retrieved to generate a coherent and informed response. This is not merely a regurgitation of all the data retrieved but an amalgamation of insights relevant to the context of your query.Using RAGI will use our sponsor, Kindo, to demonstrate how I use RAG because this is the environment in which I work habitually. However, these examples can be used in a variety of AI orchestrators. Standard chat interfaces, such as app.whiterabbitneo.com, do not have these capabilities.ChatTo demonstrate the difference between using RAG and not, I’ve asked Kindo’s WhiteRabbitNeo 2.5 32B (Beta) model the same question with and without RAG in Kindo’s chat: “analyze recent phishing email campaigns targeting healthcare organizations, detailing the most common tactics used, potential vulnerabilities exploited, and recommended mitigation strategies?”In the first image below, no chat tools are enabled, meaning that WhiteRabbitNeo is not using RAG and does not have access to real-time information. Therefore, it can only give a generic response on how healthcare organizations can prevent phishing attacks.WhiteRabbitNeo, used within the Kindo chat, responds without the use of RAG tools.In the following images, I’ve enabled web search (an implementation of RAG, accessed by clicking on “tools” and toggling the appropriate switch) before asking the same question as above. At the top of WhiteRabbitNeo’s response, you can see the web pages it references in its response. Since WhiteRabbitNeo has access to real-time information this time, it was able to give a more relevant response by referencing recent cyberattacks within the healthcare industry.WhiteRabbitNeo responds to the same query, this time with web search enabled.In addition to the web search tool, Kindo offers URL search, which allows you to search any URLs included in your query. Library search is also available, which searches any files uploaded to your Kindo library or accessible through one of your integrations, such as Google Drive. URL and Library search can be turned on by toggling their respective switches after clicking “tools.” You can also attach specific files for the LLM to reference in its response by clicking on the “plus” icon next to the query entry field. Check out this video for more information on Kindo’s search tools and using RAG in Kindo.These tools can be used to dictate the sources the LLM uses to synthesize its response. For example, if you have specific log files or other recon information you’ve gathered, you can upload these files to your Kindo library or link them via URL so that the model can analyze the documents and provide relevant insight.In the image below, I’ve uploaded a .txt file containing a Vault Agent’s sidecar logs to my Kindo library by clicking the plus sign next to the prompt field, then upload file in the top right corner of the pop-up window. Next, I asked WhiteRabbitNeo to provide context on the file, which it could do thanks to RAG.WhiteRabbitNeo analyzes a Vault Agent’s sidecar logs from a .txt file.Using WhiteRabbitNeo with the Kindo Coding PluginSince the Kindo Coding Plugin is a configuration of the Continue extension in VS Code and JetBrains, the RAG implementation options within the plugin are offered through Continue.The plugin automatically provides the ability to attach specific files, folders, or your entire codebase to your query using the appropriate @ command. The LLM will then compare your query to the embeddings of the attached files to determine what information is relevant to your question. This documentation from Continue goes into more detail regarding the @ command.Continue also provides the ability for users to create their own vector databases and configure their own custom RAG system. For those who wish to explore this further, I’ve included a link to the Continue documentation on this here. This document provides a step-by-step guide to building a custom RAG system to use in Continue (and the Kindo Coding Plugin).RAG represents a significant advancement in AI, enabling LLMs to access and utilize information outside their training dataset for enhanced accuracy and contextual understanding, allowing security professionals to analyze recon and get up-to-date information about vulnerabilities. Now that I better understand RAG, I have found myself getting more out of my AI interactions by attaching my own documents and links to search queries. I’m excited to continue to use RAG to help find information on security methodologies as I conduct research. Have you already tried using RAG with your AI queries? If not, what are you planning to try out with it first?

posts.title: How To: Locally Host WhiteRabbitNeo in Ollama
posts.subtitle: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with Ollama
posts.description: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with Ollama
posts.content: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with OllamaBy Bailey WilliamsThis is the second of a three-part series about how to host your own LLM. Today we are discussing how to locally host your own model on Ollama. Previously we discussed hosting on LM Studio and our next article will be about setting up your own rig using an NVIDIA Orin.Most people interact with AI models through official interfaces, but did you know you can also host your own model locally? Hosting your own model not only allows you to get hands-on experience with AI infrastructure and learn the intricacies of model deployment and management, but it can also allow more flexibility than using the official interfaces. For example, you do not have the same usage limits that you may face on official interfaces. Most importantly, for people and organizations with unreliable internet access or wishing to use the model with sensitive information, locally hosting your model allows you to operate the model without an internet connection.Let’s walk through how to locally host WhiteRabbitNeo using the Ollama application.Why WhiteRabbitNeo?WhiteRabbitNeo is the #1 uncensored, open-source AI model for red and blue team cybersecurity. It has a software engineering base and has been trained on a wide range of red team cybersecurity data, making it the perfect pair for any cybersecurity enthusiast, from the n00b to the seasoned hacker, and like I said in the first post, I use it to teach myself how to hack for CTF challenges.Why choose Ollama?Ollama allows you to chat with models directly in the command line, which you may prefer over switching back and forth between other programs. Ollama also allows you to locally run any AI model available on HuggingFace, as long as your computer can support it.If you haven’t already, view the supported GPU requirements for Ollama on their GitHub repository https://github.com/ollama/ollama/blob/main/docs/gpu.md. Answers to issues encountered running Ollama may also be found in the repository.Once you’ve verified that your computer can support Ollama, open your terminal and go to the directory where you want to download Ollama (~/Downloads is a good place) and install Ollama.cd ~/Downloads (or other directory)curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | shFor more information on installing Ollama, visit Ollama’s website.Steps to locally hosting WhiteRabbitNeo in Ollama:Open a terminal and go to the directory where you want the model to be downloaded (~/Downloads is a good place).cd ~/Downloads (or other directory)Install GitHub Large File Storage, which helps handle larger files.sudo apt-get install git-lfs#If this command doesn't work, try 'git lfs install'Go to https://huggingface.co/WhiteRabbitNeo and choose the model you wish to download. From the model page, click on the three dots on the right side of the screen and then click Clone Repository.You can also search “WhiteRabbitNeo” and click on WhiteRabbitNeo under Organizations.Click on the 3 dots on the model card then clone repository.The pop-up screen will remind you to install GitHub Large File Storage and give you the command to download the repository. Copy the command using the copy icon and paste it into the same command line you’ve been using. It may take a few minutes for the repository to clone.If you encounter an error message after running the command, try the following steps:#Identify the name of the model directoryls#Remove any model files that were downloaded.rm -r [name of model directory]  #Approve any override requests using Y#Retry cloning the directorygit-lfs clone [model repository URL link from Hugging Face]You now have the option to run the model using .safetensors or to convert the model files to .gguf and then run the model using the .gguf file. I will first go through the steps to convert the files to .gguf, if you wish to run using .safetensors (a much shorter process), jump here..gguf files are model files that are optimized for faster loading. You can learn more about .gguf files on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/gguf.safetensors are secure model files that are much faster than PyTorch. You can learn more about .safetensors files on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/docs/safetensors/indexConverting to a .gguf fileClone the llama.cpp GitHub repository.git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cppChange to the llama.cpp directory.cd llama.cppSet up a virtual environment.'PyTorch does not currently support beyond Python 3.12, meaning newer versions of Python will not support some of the commands below.To circumvent this, I downloaded Python version 3.12.9 from https://www.python.org/downloads/. Once it is installed, run the following commands:'python3 --version #If it says Python 3.13.0, run the commandalias python3='python3.12', #then run python3 - version again. #It should now read Python 3.12.9.Once you have the correct Python version installed, set up the virtual environment.python3 -m venv path/to/venvsource path/to/venv/bin/activateNext, install the required Python dependencies.pip install -r requirements.txtConvert the model to .ggufpython3 convert_hf_to_gguf.py [full path to WhiteRabbitNeo modeldirectory]#To get the full path to the WhiteRabbitNeo model directory:cd ~/Downloads #or whichever directory you started inls #to see the name of the WhiteRabbitNeo directorycd [name of WhiteRabbitNeo directory]pwd #this result is the full pathname#To return back to llama.cpp now that you have the pathname:cd ..cd llama.cppA new .gguf file should now be in the WhiteRabbitNeo model directory. To proceed, you’ll need the full pathname to that file.The full name of the .gguf file should be in the last line of the conversion output following “Model successfully exported to,” but if not you can find it with the following steps:cd [full pathname of WhiteRabbitNeo directory]ls #copy the .gguf filenameDeactivate the virtual environment.deactivateChange into the directory you cloned the WhiteRabbitNeo repository into.cd ~/Downloads #or whichever directory you started inCreate a model file.nano modelfile#Inside the file write:FROM ./[name of WhiteRabbitNeo directory]#do not include the brackets#To exit the file:press Control + Xpress "Y" to savepress "Enter" to accept the filenameImport the model (this may take a few minutes).ollama create [name you want for the model] -f modelfile --quantize q4_0Chat with the model.ollama run [model name you chose]Running using .safetensorsThese steps pick up after cloning the repository.Create a model file inside of the current directory. Ensure that the model directory is nested inside of the current directory.#To ensure the model directory is nested:ls #you should see the model directory listednano modelfile#Inside the file write:FROM ./[name of the .gguf file created by the conversion]#do not include the brackets#To exit the file:press Control + Xpress "Y" to savepress "Enter" to accept the filenameCreate the model (this may take a few minutes).ollama create [name you want for the model]Chat with the model.ollama run [model name you chose]Any questions?Contact us through the WhiteRabbitNeo server: https://discord.gg/8Ynkrcbk92

posts.title: How To: Locally Host WhiteRabbitNeo in LM Studio
posts.subtitle: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with LM Studio
posts.description: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with LM Studio
posts.content: A Step-by-Step Guide to Locally Hosting WhiteRabbitNeo with LM StudioBy Bailey WilliamsThis is the first of a three-part series about how to host your own LLM. In future articles, we will discuss hosting on Ollama and setting up your own rig using an NVIDIA Orin with Docker.Most people interact with AI models through official interfaces, but did you know you can also host your own model locally? Hosting your own model not only allows you to get hands-on experience with AI infrastructure and learn the intricacies of model deployment and management, but it can also allow more flexibility than using the official interfaces. For example, you do not have the same usage limits that you may face on official interfaces. Most importantly, for people and organizations with unreliable internet access or wishing to use the model with sensitive information, locally hosting your model allows you to operate the model without an internet connection.Let’s walk through how to locally host WhiteRabbitNeo using the LM Studio application.Why WhiteRabbitNeo?WhiteRabbitNeo is the #1 uncensored, open-source AI model for red & blue team cybersecurity,I particularly like WhiteRabbitNeo because it has a software engineering base and has been trained on a wide range of red team cybersecurity data, making it the perfect pair for any cybersecurity enthusiast, from the n00b to the seasoned hacker.I am using it to hone my CTF skills, but WhiteRabbitNeo also helps red teamers and bug bounty hunters craft the perfect exploit and identify vulnerabilities in code.Now let’s jump into how to locally host WhiteRabbitNeo in LM Studio.Why LM Studio?LM Studio allows you to download and locally host models from HuggingFace. Many users find it easier than Ollama because it has a GUI and doesn’t involve the command line. LM Studio also provides a chat interface, which you may prefer over accessing the model via the command line.If you haven’t already, verify the system requirements for downloading LM Studio and download the version of LM Studio that is compatible with your device. You can search for the download instructions or find them using the links below:Download LM Studio: https://lmstudio.ai/downloadLM Studio System Requirements: https://lmstudio.ai/docs/system-requirementsOnce LM Studio is installed, open the application.Steps to locally hosting WhiteRabbitNeo in LM Studio:Click the magnifying glass icon on the left sidebar to open the Mission Control panel. Mission Control is similar to a marketplace, where you can search Hugging Face for .GGUF files.An important thing to note is that not all models available on the Hugging Face website are in the .GGUF format. .GGUF files are model files that are optimized for faster loading. You can learn more about .GGUF files on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/ggufUsing the search bar at the top of the Mission Control window, type WhiteRabbitNeo, or the specific WhiteRabbitNeo model you wish to download; I am using “WhiteRabbit 33B v1” by Isonium, but there are many different models available. Then, click on the gray Search button.Select the model you want to download from the search results and click the green Download button to start the process. Depending on the download size, this may take 10–20 minutes.Once the model is downloaded, click Load Model from either the Downloads window or the Download Completed! pop-up. This will automatically open a new chat with the downloaded model.This chat window should automatically open after the model is loaded.Any questions?Contact us through the WhiteRabbitNeo server: https://discord.gg/8Ynkrcbk92

posts.title: WhiteRabbitNeo AI: Follow the white rabbit to CTF success
posts.subtitle: How to use WhiteRabbitNeo to augment your Def Con CTF experience
posts.description: How to use WhiteRabbitNeo to augment your Def Con CTF experience
posts.content: How to use WhiteRabbitNeo to augment your Def Con CTF experienceCTFs are the crown jewel in the exciting cacophony of things to learn, people to see, and things to do that is Def Con. By the same token, you also want to optimize your time at Def Con, which may mean not spending all your time working on a CTF. I created this guide to help hackers use AI to maximize their time on CTFs to combat the high-pressure, overstimulating environment and get the most out of their time at Def Con.WhiteRabbitNeo, an AI model trained on security data, allows you to focus more on developing your offensive hacking skills and less on scouring the internet. Using it will let you get the most out of your CTF experience whether you are a beginner or hold a Black Badge. We’ll cover many applications of WhiteRabbitNeo, including: explaining complex or new-to-you topics, answering coding questions, building tools, analyzing network traffic, and code analysis.Explaining Complex TopicsWhether you are a beginner hacker or highly experienced, the essence of hacking requires you to learn about new security concepts on the fly. Having access to a tool trained on the best available security information means that you have an AI tutor to get you up to speed no matter the topic.WhiteRabbitNeo can explain any security topic, including complex ones like binary exploitation, in an easy to understand way, allowing you to spend less time wading through technical articles and more time applying the information you just learned.One way WhiteRabbitNeo makes complex information accessible is by explaining concepts at any level. In the example below, I asked WhiteRabbitNeo to explain binary exploitation at a ninth grade reading level, including multiple relative analogies to help you better understand the concepts. Once I have this concept down, I can ask it a follow up question or ask it to explain again with increasing nuance until I understand exactly what I need to know.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/rjaIbZZWhile Jeopardy-style CTF questions are some of the easiest CTF questions, they eat up a lot of valuable time if you have to search for the answer. Instead, ask WhiteRabbitNeo these questions and it will provide the answer in seconds without wasting your time scrolling through search results. Then, you can spend all the time you saved on harder, more rewarding questions.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/jpNDBneAnswering Coding QuestionsWhiteRabbitNeo is instructed to give code-first answers. This means that if it can create code to respond to a prompt, it will. This makes it great for answering questions about code, such as questions about Kali Linux applications. Use WhiteRabbitNeo to determine the Linux commands best suited for your intentions as well as to explain different tools offered by Kali Linux.For example, if you need to list all files in a Linux directory, including hidden ones, simply ask WhiteRabbitNeo for the correct command.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/6ko1VvYEven better, ask complex Kali usage questions, such as explaining how to set up a reverse-shell connection using Metasploit, as seen below.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/YhDTmsjNew to Kali and need help figuring out what tool to use? Ask WhiteRabbitNeo which Kali tool is most appropriate for your challenge. For example, if a CTF challenge asks you to find hidden web objects, simply ask WhiteRabbitNeo for the best Kali tool to use and how to apply it.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/rL7uNfEWhiteRabbitNeo can also answer more general Kali application questions, such as the best Kali tools to receive information about security vulnerabilities.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/ku9P9i5Building ToolsWhiteRabbitNeo also saves you time by creating custom scripts for tools required to solve challenges. Instead of searching the web to find the correct tool or trying multiple tools until you find a good version, simply use WhiteRabbitNeo and generate your scripts all in one place.In the example below, WhiteRabbitNeo creates a port scanner in Python that I am then able to use to find vulnerabilities on a network.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/yzIq5JcCheck out my previous article that dives deeper into creating custom offensive hacking tools with WhiteRabbitNeo here.Analyzing Network TrafficEvery CTF requires you to analyze and identify anomalies in network traffic at some point. While WhiteRabbitNeo does not currently support .pcap files, meaning it cannot analyze complete Wireshark captures, it can still help you analyze network captures. Just paste whatever network traffic you need to analyze into WhiteRabbitNeo. In the example below, WhiteRabbitNeo explains the characteristics of 3 frames of a fragmented and reassembled packet.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/4O5xKrKAnother network analysis capability of WhiteRabbitNeo is reading IP addresses and identifying any malicious activity. Here, WhiteRabbitNeo identifies the malware Emotet, and explains how it spreads and who it commonly targets. It then provided a list of ways to protect against Emotet, complete with Splunk rules to automate the process.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/kNXPluACode AnalysisI like to combine WhiteRabbitNeo with the Kindo coding copilot in VS Code for more powerful offensive hacking applications. Within the plugin, WhiteRabbitNeo is able to give feedback on code and even help debug complex code. In this example, WhiteRabbitNeo summarizes the functions of the provided code and its related files.As you can see, code review is a pretty powerful application of WhiteRabbitNeo. Not only does it detect vulnerabilities, it also provides recommendations on how to best secure your code.While both these features are available with the standard WhiteRabbitNeo chat, the Kindo plugin in VS Code is more user friendly and allows for more complex analysis. In the example below, WhiteRabbitNeo analyzes a program in Ruby and recommends examples for hardening the code against potential vulnerabilities.LimitationsWhile WhiteRabbitNeo is a powerful tool, it is not the answer for all your CTF needs. WhiteRabbitNeo is limited in the types of files it supports, and does not currently support images or .pcap files. This prevents WhiteRabbitNeo from solving steganography challenges and conducting in depth network traffic analysis on its own, but it is still a helpful sidekick.As discussed in the networking section, after pasting in sections of .pcap files, WhiteRabbitNeo will analyze them and answer whatever questions you have. In addition, it is able to generate Wireshark commands to filter data and make it easier for you to analyze network captures.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/ralSteeAdditionally, WhiteRabbitNeo provides guidance on how to solve these challenges, and is a good point of reference for new-to-you challenges. In the example below, WhiteRabbitNeo provides multiple ways to solve a steganography challenge, including code that can be run in an IDE.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/LGESIyOBest WhiteRabbitNeo prompts for CTFsLike any AI model, WhiteRabbitNeo works best when given detailed but succinct prompts to work with. To help you master this learning curve, check out this post about the best prompts to use in a CTF setting.

posts.title: Writing Prompts with WhiteRabbitNeo AI
posts.subtitle: AI is only as good as the prompts you give, here are a few prompts to get you started with WhiteRabbitNeo.
posts.description: AI is only as good as the prompts you give, here are a few prompts to get you started with WhiteRabbitNeo.
posts.content: AI is only as good as the prompts you give, here are a few prompts to get you started with WhiteRabbitNeo.Writing prompts for AI models doesn’t have to be complicated, but you gotta get it right. They understand natural language the best, so write just like you would write directed to a human. Be clear and concise in your questions and try out different ways of phrasing a prompt. If the AI doesn’t give you the response you wanted, don’t be afraid to push the model to do better. Tell the model what you didn’t like or what about its response didn’t work, and ask the model to be more specific or explain things in more detail.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/HRh9hJ1Check out these prompts and example outputs from the code-first​​ AI model WhiteRabbitNeo, which is trained on the largest security-centric training set, to see how you can utilize WhiteRabbitNeo in your daily tasks.Prompt: “Construct an attack against [Tomcat CVE] in Python”Prompt: “Are there any CVEs for these [services]”Shows you any known exploits for older, unpatched services.Prompt: “Write attack code for CVE-2018–1321”Prompt: “Are there any vulnerabilities in this code?”Prompt: “What is Emotet and how do I detect these IP addresses in Splunk rules?”

posts.title: Port Scanning, Brute Forcing, Decrypting, Oh My!
posts.subtitle: 3 ways I automate my hacking process with WhiteRabbitNeo
posts.description: 3 ways I automate my hacking process with WhiteRabbitNeo
posts.content: 3 ways I automate my hacking process with WhiteRabbitNeoSolving CTF (Capture the Flag) challenges is thrilling, but the road to that solution is incredibly tedious and time consuming. Especially when one is new to CTFs, figuring out what the vulnerability is and then crafting and executing a plan for how to exploit the vuln is a long, and dead-end prone process. When competing in CTFs, time is of the essence, so it is important to avoid spinning your wheels searching for the best tool or getting bogged down in the details of crafting the perfect attack.I use WhiteRabbitNeo to simplify this process. It lets me develop personalized tools and scripts that fit my hacking needs without taking time to find free tools online or write them from scratch. WhiteRabbitNeo is capable of developing a wide range of tools and scripts, including port scanners, brute forcing programs, wordlists, complex reconnaissance and exploitation programs, and decryption algorithms in seconds. These tools are then easily copied into my preferred IDE, VS Code, and executed.WhiteRabbitNeo has a software engineering foundation trained on the largest security-centric training set, which makes it an ideal companion for any hacker. As a code-first model, WhiteRabbitNeo often produces a program first, before going into an explanation of the program and the question asked. At the end of the program, WhiteRabbitNeo provides example use cases for it, which makes the programs even easier for hackers to use.Port ScanningScanning for open ports is an easy way to identify potential paths into a system. My preferred network scanner is Nmap, so I asked WhiteRabbitNeo to use Nmap to create a port scanner.While you can use Nmap directly from the terminal, using a script allows you to personalize the output and can take less time then determining the correct Nmap flags to use. After WhiteRabbitNeo generates the script, I simply use the copy icon on the top right of the Python script to easily copy the program and paste it into VS Code.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/lu95Gm2Since I did not already have Nmap installed, there was an error when I first ran the program. To quickly resolve this, I asked WhiteRabbitNeo in Kindo’s Coding Copilot how to install Nmap, which I then did from VS Code’s terminal.Brute Force AttacksAfter identifying open ports on a server, the next step in a CTF is often to visit the different ports and find vulnerabilities that can be exploited to gain access to the system. WhiteRabbitNeo is able to generate tools for password attacks, including directory brute force attacks that can help gain access to hidden directories. In the following example, WhiteRabbitNeo has created a script for a tool that executes directory brute force attacks in Bash.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/dQZrNvkWhile some CTF challenges may provide wordlists to use in attacks, WhiteRabbitNeo is capable of generating them if you need one. For example, this wordlist contains common directory names, to be used in the previous directory brute forcing program.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/Sgl3XJrAutomating ReconnaissanceWhile at Def Con, I want to be able to take in the whole experience, not spend all my time completing time-consuming and complex CTF challenges. Using WhiteRabbitNeo, I can automate much of this process, reducing the amount of expertise required to perform these tasks.WhiteRabbitNeo is able to write complex scripts, which utilize multiple tools to accomplish your goal. For example, the following program runs an Nmap scan, Gobuster, Nikto, and Nuclei, which allows a hacker to automate their reconnaissance process by scanning the target IP address, listing open ports and directories, and identifying potential vulnerabilities the hacker is then able to exploit.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/fzKzS3YDecryption ToolsCryptography questions are some of the easiest CTF questions to solve from a technical standpoint, however, they take up a large amount of your time if you have to search for different decryption websites every time. Instead, automate the process with WhiteRabbitNeo and ask it to write your decryption algorithms.This allows hackers to stay on the same webpage while completing cryptography challenges rather than having to search the internet for different websites. To use the algorithms, simply copy the code and paste it into your preferred IDE. WhiteRabbitNeo even provides example use cases, which are easily customizable to decrypt the intended ciphertext.Link to full chat: https://www.whiterabbitneo.com/share/BN5Cv8tConclusionWhile these 3 tools barely scratch the surface of the ever-evolving hacking landscape, they provide a great introduction to the ways WhiteRabbitNeo can help you automate your hacking processes. These tools can not only save you time, but also make you a better hacker as you become more comfortable with phrasing challenges and thinking through the processes. I’ll be at Def Con this weekend, and you can bet I will be using these strategies!P.S. If WhiteRabbitNeo does not deliver what you want at first, don’t be afraid to push the model to do better. Just tell it what you don’t like or didn’t work and ask the model to be more specific or to make it more powerful or to explain things with more detail.
